credit_headline.narrative: Bank credit dipped ₹1.5L Cr to ₹212.1L Cr in April 2026, the first decline after six periods of growth, but remains near the all-time high of ₹213.6L Cr set in March. The pullback is a post-year-end correction, not a trend reversal: YoY growth eased to 16.0% from 17.1%, still in the upper range of the historical window. Non-food credit — agriculture, industry, services, retail — drives 99% of the system and mirrors the headline trend, while food credit surged to a record ₹1.04L Cr on seasonal procurement. All four main sectors continue to grow, confirming broad-based momentum. The FY26 acceleration (10.0% → 17.1%, a 7.1pp step-up) remains the sharpest inflection on record, with banks deploying ₹31.2L Cr of net new credit, the highest annual add in this dataset. Lenders should watch whether May resumes growth or signals a deeper slowdown — either way, credit is expanding faster than at any prior point in this window, intensifying competition for quality assets and demanding close scrutiny of underwriting standards and portfolio concentration risk.

sibc-bank-credit-abs.title: Bank credit dipped to ₹212.1L Cr — first decline in 7 periods
sibc-bank-credit-abs.observation: Total bank credit outstanding fell to ₹212.1L Cr in April 2026, down ₹1.5L Cr from the March 2026 peak of ₹213.6L Cr.
sibc-bank-credit-abs.direction: Volume declined ₹1.5L Cr period-on-period (₹213.6L Cr → ₹212.1L Cr), breaking a six-period climb from ₹178.7L Cr in January 2025.
chain: Bank credit fell ₹1.5L Cr in April 2026 after reaching ₹213.6L Cr in March 2026, the first decline in this dataset.
The drop follows the March year-end peak, when banks typically book maximum disbursements to close the fiscal year.
April marks the start of FY27; the pullback likely reflects post-year-end portfolio adjustments and repayments settling.
Despite the dip, the ₹212.1L Cr level remains in the upper range of the historical window, well above the ₹178.7L Cr starting point.
sibc-bank-credit-abs.inference: The April decline is a post-year-end correction, not a trend reversal. Credit remains elevated at ₹212.1L Cr — lenders should watch whether May sustains this level or resumes the climb. If growth restarts, the FY27 trajectory will mirror FY26's aggressive pace.

sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.title: Bank credit grew 10.0% in FY25, 17.1% in FY26 — 7.1pp step-up
sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.observation: Bank credit grew 10.0% in FY25 and 17.1% in FY26, a 7.1 percentage point acceleration.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.direction: The full-year growth rate jumped from 10.0% in the year ending March 2025 to 17.1% in the year ending March 2026, the largest annual step-up in this dataset.
chain: Bank credit grew 10.0% in FY25 and 17.1% in FY26, a 7.1 percentage point acceleration.
This is the sharpest full-year inflection on record, signaling a decisive shift from single-digit to mid-teens growth.
Lenders who scaled origination capacity in FY25 captured outsized share in FY26; those who lagged now face a widening competitive gap.
The acceleration is locked at March year-end and will not change until March 2027, when FY27 closes.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.inference: The credit cycle has turned decisively. Banks and NBFCs that built underwriting and distribution muscle in FY25 are now reaping compounding gains. Laggards must act fast or risk permanent share loss in a high-growth environment.

sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.title: Banks deployed ₹31.2L Cr in FY26 — highest annual add on record
sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.observation: Banks deployed ₹31.2L Cr of net new credit in FY26, the highest annual addition in this window.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.direction: This figure has remained constant at ₹31.2L Cr across all seven periods, as it reflects the full-year tally locked at March 2026 year-end.
chain: Banks deployed ₹31.2L Cr of net new credit in FY26, the highest annual addition in this dataset.
This is a full-year metric set at March 2026 and unchanged since; it will reset only at March 2027 when FY27 closes.
The ₹31.2L Cr add represents a 17.1% expansion on the prior year's base, the fastest pace on record here.
At this scale, underwriting standards and portfolio concentration risk deserve close scrutiny.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.inference: The system added more credit in one year than in any prior period tracked. Lenders must audit asset quality and capital adequacy headroom — growth at this velocity can mask emerging stress in weaker pockets.

sibc-bank-credit-yoy.title: Bank credit YoY eased to 16.0% — still in upper range
sibc-bank-credit-yoy.observation: Bank credit grew 16.0% year-on-year in April 2026, down from 17.1% in March 2026.
sibc-bank-credit-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated 1.1 percentage points (17.1% → 16.0%), the first slowdown after a three-period acceleration from 14.6% in January 2026.
chain: Bank credit grew 16.0% year-on-year in April 2026, down from 17.1% in March 2026.
The 1.1 percentage point deceleration follows the March year-end peak, when banks typically front-load disbursements.
Despite the slowdown, 16.0% remains at the top of the historical range (p75 = 16.0%), well above the 10.0% low in March 2025.
The April dip in absolute volume (₹212.1L Cr vs ₹213.6L Cr) explains the rate moderation.
sibc-bank-credit-yoy.inference: The April slowdown is a post-year-end normalization, not a trend break. Growth remains robust at 16.0% — watch whether May resumes acceleration or settles into a mid-teens plateau. Either way, credit is expanding faster than in any prior period here.

sibc-food-credit-abs.title: Food credit surged to ₹1.04L Cr — highest level on record
sibc-food-credit-abs.observation: Food credit outstanding jumped to ₹1.04L Cr in April 2026, up ₹0.34L Cr from ₹0.70L Cr in March 2026.
sibc-food-credit-abs.direction: Volume rose ₹0.34L Cr period-on-period (₹0.70L Cr → ₹1.04L Cr), the largest single-period increase in this dataset and a new all-time high.
chain: Food credit outstanding jumped to ₹1.04L Cr in April 2026, up ₹0.34L Cr from ₹0.70L Cr in March 2026.
This is the highest level in the seven-period window, surpassing the prior peak of ₹0.89L Cr in January 2026.
Food credit is volatile and small relative to the ₹212.1L Cr system total, accounting for less than 0.5% of bank credit.
The April surge likely reflects seasonal procurement cycles as the new crop year begins.
sibc-food-credit-abs.inference: Food credit is a rounding error in the ₹212L Cr system, but the April spike signals active procurement financing. Lenders in agri-commodity finance should watch for repayment cycles as the season progresses.

sibc-food-credit-yoy.title: Food credit YoY slowed to 66.4% — still elevated vs history
sibc-food-credit-yoy.observation: Food credit grew 66.4% year-on-year in April 2026, down from 92.4% in March 2026.
sibc-food-credit-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated 25.9 percentage points (92.4% → 66.4%), the sharpest single-period slowdown in this dataset, but remains near the median of the historical range.
chain: Food credit grew 66.4% year-on-year in April 2026, down from 92.4% in March 2026.
The 25.9 percentage point deceleration is the largest drop in this window, driven by a rising base as April 2025 comparisons normalize.
Despite the slowdown, 66.4% is still elevated relative to the p25 of 34.2%, reflecting continued strong procurement financing.
The absolute level of ₹1.04L Cr is at a record high, so the rate moderation is purely a base effect.
sibc-food-credit-yoy.inference: The triple-digit growth rates are compressing as the base normalizes, but food credit remains robust in absolute terms. Expect further rate deceleration in coming periods as the low base from early 2025 rolls off.

sibc-nonfood-credit-abs.title: Non-food credit dipped to ₹211.1L Cr — first decline in 7 periods
sibc-nonfood-credit-abs.observation: Non-food credit outstanding fell to ₹211.1L Cr in April 2026, down ₹1.8L Cr from the March 2026 peak of ₹212.9L Cr.
sibc-nonfood-credit-abs.direction: Volume declined ₹1.8L Cr period-on-period (₹212.9L Cr → ₹211.1L Cr), breaking a six-period climb from ₹178.1L Cr in January 2025.
chain: Non-food credit fell ₹1.8L Cr in April 2026 after reaching ₹212.9L Cr in March 2026, the first decline in this dataset.
The drop mirrors the system-wide pullback, as non-food credit accounts for 99% of total bank credit.
April marks the start of FY27; the decline likely reflects post-year-end portfolio adjustments and repayments settling after the March disbursement push.
Despite the dip, the ₹211.1L Cr level remains in the upper range of the historical window.
sibc-nonfood-credit-abs.inference: Non-food credit drives the system-wide trend. The April decline is a post-year-end correction, not a reversal. Lenders should watch whether May resumes growth or signals a deeper slowdown in agriculture, industry, services, and retail lending.

sibc-nonfood-credit-fy-delta.title: Non-food credit added ₹30.8L Cr in FY26 — record annual deployment
sibc-nonfood-credit-fy-delta.observation: Banks deployed ₹30.8L Cr of net new non-food credit in FY26, the highest annual addition in this window.
sibc-nonfood-credit-fy-delta.direction: This figure has remained constant at ₹30.8L Cr across all seven periods, as it reflects the full-year tally locked at March 2026 year-end.
chain: Banks deployed ₹30.8L Cr of net new non-food credit in FY26, the highest annual addition in this dataset.
Non-food credit accounts for 99% of the ₹31.2L Cr system-wide add, confirming it is the primary driver of credit expansion.
This is a full-year metric set at March 2026 and unchanged since; it will reset only at March 2027 when FY27 closes.
The ₹30.8L Cr deployment is broad-based across agriculture, industry, services, and retail — a positive signal for economic activity.
sibc-nonfood-credit-fy-delta.inference: Non-food credit is where the real lending action is. The ₹30.8L Cr add signals aggressive deployment across productive sectors, but lenders must watch for pockets of over-leverage as growth sustains at this scale.

sibc-nonfood-credit-yoy.title: Non-food credit YoY eased to 15.8% — still in upper range
sibc-nonfood-credit-yoy.observation: Non-food credit grew 15.8% year-on-year in April 2026, down from 16.9% in March 2026.
sibc-nonfood-credit-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated 1.1 percentage points (16.9% → 15.8%), the first slowdown after a three-period acceleration from 14.4% in January 2026.
chain: Non-food credit grew 15.8% year-on-year in April 2026, down from 16.9% in March 2026.
The 1.1 percentage point deceleration mirrors the system-wide slowdown, driven by the April dip in absolute volume (₹211.1L Cr vs ₹212.9L Cr).
Despite the slowdown, 15.8% remains at the top of the historical range (p75 = 15.8%), well above the 9.9% low in March 2025.
Non-food credit is accelerating in lockstep with total bank credit, confirming broad-based momentum across sectors.
sibc-nonfood-credit-yoy.inference: The April slowdown is a post-year-end normalization, not a trend break. Non-food credit remains robust at 15.8% — lenders should expect pricing pressure and margin compression in competitive segments as growth sustains above historical norms.

sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.title: All 4 main sectors grew YoY — breadth unchanged for 7 periods
sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.observation: All four main credit sectors — agriculture, industry, services, retail — posted positive year-on-year growth in April 2026.
sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.direction: Breadth has held at 4 out of 4 sectors for the entire seven-period window, with no sector slipping into contraction.
chain: All four main credit sectors posted positive year-on-year growth in April 2026, unchanged from March 2026.
Breadth has remained at 4 out of 4 sectors for seven consecutive periods, the longest streak in this dataset.
This confirms credit expansion is broad-based, not concentrated in one or two segments.
No sector is being starved, which reduces systemic risk but intensifies competition for quality assets across the board.
sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.inference: Credit expansion is firing on all cylinders. Lenders face intense competition for quality borrowers in every sector — expect pricing pressure, margin compression, and heightened underwriting discipline to sustain portfolio quality.

psl.narrative: PSL growth is bifurcating sharply: Housing (39.9%), Social Infrastructure (35.3%), and Educational Loans (23.3% and strengthening) are pulling away, while Export Credit (-12.0%) and Others (-12.5%) are contracting faster. The spread widened to 52.4pp, the highest in this window, as banks concentrate capital in retail-facing, scalable PSL categories. Micro and Small Enterprises stabilised at 28.5% but shifted to active status — growth is no longer accelerating. Four segments are strengthening, signalling broad retail momentum, but corporate and export PSL remain structurally weak. For lenders, housing and social infrastructure offer the clearest path to PSL compliance and margin expansion.

sibc-psl-agri-yoy.title: PSL Agriculture at 17.3% YoY — highest on record
sibc-psl-agri-yoy.observation: PSL Agriculture credit grew 17.33% year-on-year in Apr 2026, the strongest reading in the seven-period series.
sibc-psl-agri-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 15.95% last period to 17.33%, extending a steady climb from 11.0% in Mar 2025 → 17.3% in Apr 2026.
chain: Agriculture credit grew 17.33% YoY in Apr 2026, the highest rate in this window and 1.38pp faster than last period.
The trajectory shows sustained acceleration from a low of 11.0% in Mar 2025, with no reversal across seven periods.
This is the strongest momentum in the dataset — banks are deploying capital to agriculture at an accelerating pace, likely driven by policy support and seasonal demand.
sibc-psl-agri-yoy.inference: Agriculture lending is in a structural uptrend. Banks should expect sustained demand through the kharif cycle — this is the time to expand exposure and capture market share in a segment with policy tailwinds.

sibc-psl-education-yoy.title: PSL Education Loans hold at 23.3% — breakout sustained
sibc-psl-education-yoy.observation: PSL Educational Loans grew 23.27% year-on-year in Apr 2026, holding near the upper end of the range.
sibc-psl-education-yoy.direction: Growth was near zero through Mar 2025 (0.9%), then surged to 23.5% in Jan 2026 and has held above 23% for four straight periods (23.5% → 24.0% → 23.0% → 23.3%).
chain: Education lending grew 23.27% YoY in Apr 2026, the fourth consecutive period above 23% after a year-long stall at near-zero growth.
The breakout from 0.9% in Mar 2025 to 23.5% in Jan 2026 marks a structural shift, not a cyclical bounce.
Banks have re-engaged with student loans — this is a sustained trend, likely driven by demand recovery and policy support for education financing.
sibc-psl-education-yoy.inference: Education lending has broken out and stabilised at high growth. This is a structural opportunity — banks should scale origination capacity and expect sustained demand as the segment re-rates.

sibc-psl-export-yoy.title: PSL Export Credit at -12.0% — contraction deepens again
sibc-psl-export-yoy.observation: PSL Export Credit contracted 12.02% year-on-year in Apr 2026, reversing the prior period's improvement.
sibc-psl-export-yoy.direction: The contraction worsened from -8.36% last period to -12.02%, breaking a three-period easing trend from -17.2% in Jan 2026 → -8.4% in Mar 2026.
chain: Export credit contracted 12.02% YoY in Apr 2026, 3.66pp worse than the -8.36% decline last period.
The trajectory had been improving from -17.2% in Jan 2026 to -8.4% in Mar 2026, but has now reversed direction.
The renewed deterioration suggests trade finance demand remains weak — the stabilisation seen in Mar 2026 was not sustained.
sibc-psl-export-yoy.inference: Export credit is not yet stabilising — the reversal from -8.4% to -12.0% signals continued weakness in trade finance. Banks should remain cautious on export lending until a sustained recovery emerges.

sibc-psl-housing-yoy.title: PSL Housing at 39.9% YoY — record growth sustained
sibc-psl-housing-yoy.observation: PSL Housing credit grew 39.94% year-on-year in Apr 2026, the highest reading in the seven-period series.
sibc-psl-housing-yoy.direction: Growth was negative through Mar 2025 (-1.1%), then surged to 37.9% in Jan 2026 and has accelerated further to 39.8% in Mar 2026 → 39.9% in Apr 2026.
chain: Housing credit grew 39.94% YoY in Apr 2026, the strongest rate in this window and near the prior period's 39.80%.
The trajectory shows a structural break from negative growth in Mar 2025 (-1.1%) to sustained expansion above 37% for four straight periods.
This is the standout PSL segment — banks are aggressively deploying capital to affordable housing, and the momentum shows no sign of slowing.
sibc-psl-housing-yoy.inference: Housing is the engine of PSL growth. Banks should maximise exposure to this segment — demand is structural, not cyclical, and the trajectory supports continued aggressive deployment.

sibc-psl-medium-yoy.title: PSL Medium Enterprises at 17.2% — slowest in window
sibc-psl-medium-yoy.observation: PSL Medium Enterprises credit grew 17.24% year-on-year in Apr 2026, the weakest reading in the seven-period series.
sibc-psl-medium-yoy.direction: Growth has decelerated steadily from 22.6% in Mar 2025 → 18.9% in Jan 2026 → 17.9% in Mar 2026 → 17.2% in Apr 2026.
chain: Medium enterprises grew 17.24% YoY in Apr 2026, the slowest rate in this window and 0.66pp below last period.
The trajectory shows sustained deceleration from a peak of 22.6% in Mar 2025, with no reversal across seven periods.
While Micro & Small are surging at 28.5%, Medium enterprises are losing momentum — banks may be shifting focus to smaller borrowers or seeing weaker demand from mid-sized firms.
sibc-psl-medium-yoy.inference: Medium enterprises are losing share to Micro & Small. Banks should assess whether this reflects demand weakness or strategic reallocation — if the former, credit quality may be deteriorating in this segment.

sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.title: PSL MSME grew 29.4% in FY26 vs 13.4% in FY25 — 15.9pp step-up
sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.observation: PSL MSME credit grew 13.4% in the year ending Mar 2025 and 29.4% in the year ending Mar 2026 — a 15.9-point acceleration.
sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.direction: Full-year growth more than doubled from FY25 to FY26, marking the largest year-over-year step-up in this dataset.
chain: MSME credit grew 13.4% in FY25 and 29.4% in FY26, a 15.9-point acceleration — the largest in this window.
This is a structural inflection, not a cyclical bounce — full-year growth more than doubled in a single year.
Banks are pouring capital into small businesses, likely driven by policy support and demand recovery — this is the fastest-accelerating PSL segment.
sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.inference: MSME is the structural growth driver in PSL. Banks should expect this momentum to anchor FY27 performance — scale origination capacity and monitor credit quality as volumes surge.

sibc-psl-msme-yoy.title: PSL Micro & Small at 28.5% YoY — upper range sustained
sibc-psl-msme-yoy.observation: PSL Micro & Small enterprises credit grew 28.53% year-on-year in Apr 2026, holding in the upper range of the series.
sibc-psl-msme-yoy.direction: Growth has surged from 13.4% in Mar 2025 → 24.8% in Jan 2026 → 29.4% in Mar 2026 → 28.5% in Apr 2026, with a slight 0.83pp deceleration in the latest period.
chain: Micro & Small credit grew 28.53% YoY in Apr 2026, down 0.83pp from 29.36% last period but still in the upper range of the series.
The trajectory shows a structural break from 13.4% in Mar 2025 to sustained expansion above 24% for four straight periods.
This is the engine of PSL growth — banks are aggressively expanding exposure to the smallest borrowers, and the momentum remains strong despite the slight deceleration.
sibc-psl-msme-yoy.inference: Micro & Small is the core PSL growth segment. The slight deceleration from 29.4% to 28.5% is noise — the structural trend is intact. Banks should continue scaling exposure and monitor credit quality as volumes compound.

sibc-psl-others-yoy.title: PSL Others at -12.5% — contraction worsens after improvement
sibc-psl-others-yoy.observation: PSL Others contracted 12.45% year-on-year in Apr 2026, reversing the prior period's sharp improvement.
sibc-psl-others-yoy.direction: The contraction worsened from -9.55% last period to -12.45%, breaking a three-period easing trend from -23.9% in Jan 2026 → -9.6% in Mar 2026.
chain: The 'Others' bucket contracted 12.45% YoY in Apr 2026, 2.90pp worse than the -9.55% decline last period.
The trajectory had been improving sharply from -23.9% in Jan 2026 to -9.6% in Mar 2026, but has now reversed direction.
The renewed deterioration suggests the stabilisation seen in Mar 2026 was not sustained — miscellaneous PSL categories remain under pressure.
sibc-psl-others-yoy.inference: PSL Others is not yet stabilising — the reversal from -9.6% to -12.5% signals continued weakness in miscellaneous categories. Banks should remain cautious until a sustained recovery emerges.

sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.title: PSL Renewable Energy positive for 8 straight periods — longest streak
sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.observation: PSL Renewable Energy has posted positive year-on-year growth for 8 consecutive periods, the longest streak in this dataset.
sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.direction: The streak extended from 7 periods last time to 8 now, with no interruption since the series began.
chain: Renewable Energy has grown every period for 8 months straight, the longest run on record.
The unbroken streak since the series began signals entrenched positive momentum, not a cyclical bounce.
Banks are consistently deploying capital to green projects — this is a structural trend with policy tailwinds.
sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.inference: Renewable Energy lending is structurally entrenched in positive territory. Banks should expect sustained demand — this is a long-term opportunity with policy support and ESG tailwinds.

sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.title: PSL Renewable Energy at 15.6% — sharp deceleration continues
sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.observation: PSL Renewable Energy grew 15.64% year-on-year in Apr 2026, the slowest reading in the seven-period series.
sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated sharply from 26.01% last period to 15.64%, and from a peak of 78.3% in Mar 2025 → 62.0% in Jan 2026 → 15.6% in Apr 2026.
chain: Renewable Energy grew 15.64% YoY in Apr 2026, down 10.38pp from 26.01% last period and the slowest rate in this window.
The trajectory shows sustained deceleration from a peak of 78.3% in Mar 2025, with growth now at less than one-fifth of that level.
The segment is still growing but has lost significant momentum — this suggests normalisation after an exceptional FY25, not a structural breakdown.
sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.inference: Renewable Energy is decelerating sharply but remains in positive territory. Banks should watch whether growth stabilises above 15% or decelerates further — the 8-period positive streak is intact, but the pace is slowing.

sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.title: PSL Social Infrastructure at 35.3% — volatile rebound sustained
sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.observation: PSL Social Infrastructure grew 35.27% year-on-year in Apr 2026, accelerating sharply from 12.88% last period.
sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.direction: Growth surged from 12.88% in Mar 2026 to 35.27% in Apr 2026, a 22.39-point jump, after swinging from -1.0% in Feb 2026 to 12.9% in Mar 2026.
chain: Social Infrastructure grew 35.27% YoY in Apr 2026, up 22.39pp from 12.88% last period.
The trajectory is highly volatile — growth swung from -1.0% in Feb 2026 to 12.9% in Mar 2026 to 35.3% in Apr 2026.
The sharp acceleration suggests renewed deployment, but the erratic pattern makes it difficult to assess whether this is a sustained trend or another swing in a volatile series.
sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.inference: Social Infrastructure is growing strongly but remains highly volatile. Banks should treat this segment with caution — the 22.39pp jump is a positive signal, but the erratic trajectory warrants close monitoring before scaling exposure.

sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.title: PSL Weaker Sections at 10.7% — slowest in window
sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.observation: PSL Weaker Sections (including PSLC SF/MF) grew 10.67% year-on-year in Apr 2026, the weakest reading in the seven-period series.
sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated from 11.07% last period to 10.67%, and from a peak of 15.3% in Mar 2025 → 11.9% in Jan 2026 → 10.7% in Apr 2026.
chain: Weaker Sections grew 10.67% YoY in Apr 2026, the slowest rate in this window and 0.41pp below last period.
The trajectory shows sustained deceleration from a peak of 15.3% in Mar 2025, with no reversal across seven periods.
While Micro & Small are surging at 28.5% and Housing at 39.9%, Weaker Sections are losing momentum — banks may be prioritising higher-growth PSL categories.
sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.inference: Weaker Sections lending is losing share to Micro & Small and Housing. Banks should assess whether this reflects demand weakness or strategic reallocation — if the former, policy interventions may be needed to revive this segment.

sibc-psl-yoy-scan.title: Housing 39.9%, Micro & Small 28.5% — PSL spread widens to 52.4pp
sibc-psl-yoy-scan.observation: Housing credit grew 39.9% YoY, Social Infrastructure 35.3%, and Micro and Small Enterprises 28.5% — the three fastest-growing PSL segments.
sibc-psl-yoy-scan.direction: The spread between top and bottom performers widened from 49.4pp to 52.4pp. Export Credit contracted 12.0% (from -8.4%), Others declined 12.5% (from -9.6%). Micro and Small Enterprises shifted from strengthening to active at 28.5% (from 29.4%), while Educational Loans moved from active to strengthening at 23.3%.
chain: Housing credit grew 39.9% YoY, Social Infrastructure 35.3%, and Micro and Small Enterprises 28.5% — the three fastest-growing PSL segments.
The spread between top and bottom performers widened to 52.4pp, up from 49.4pp, as Export Credit's contraction deepened to -12.0% and Others fell to -12.5%.

Four segments are strengthening (Housing, Social Infrastructure, Educational Loans, Agriculture), four active (Micro and Small, Medium Enterprises, Renewable Energy, Weaker Sections), and two declining (Export Credit, Others).
PSL growth is polarising further: retail-facing categories (housing, education, social infrastructure) are accelerating, while corporate/export segments are contracting faster.
sibc-psl-yoy-scan.inference: The widening spread to 52.4pp confirms banks are doubling down on retail PSL — housing, education, and social infrastructure — while abandoning export and corporate categories. Micro and Small Enterprises' shift to active status at 28.5% signals momentum stabilising but no longer accelerating. Export Credit's deepening contraction to -12.0% reflects sustained trade weakness. Lenders should prioritise housing and social infrastructure for PSL compliance; export-linked PSL is structurally challenged.

retail.narrative: Personal loans are in a structural shift: gold lending at 121% YoY is reshaping the mix, while vehicles (18% YoY) and other PL (13% YoY) have inflected sharply. Housing remains the anchor at 48.2% share but is losing ground despite healthy 11.4% growth — it is being outrun by faster-growing segments. Six of nine categories are active, with credit cards strengthening at 3.8% and only consumer durables and advances against shares in contraction. The personal loan cycle is broadening beyond gold: lenders overweight in housing are missing the momentum plays. Incremental capital should flow to gold, vehicles, and other PL to capture the fastest-growing pockets.

sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.title: Credit cards below 10% YoY for 4 straight periods — record streak
sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.observation: Credit card growth has remained below 10% YoY for 4 consecutive periods, the longest such run in this dataset.
sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.direction: The sub-10% streak extended from 3 periods in Mar 2026 to 4 periods in Apr 2026.
chain: Credit card growth has stayed below 10% YoY for 4 consecutive periods, the longest streak on record.
This sustained cooling follows a sharp deceleration from 13.0% in Jan 2025 to 3.8% in Apr 2026.
The streak signals entrenched structural weakness — regulatory scrutiny and tighter underwriting have durably suppressed card origination.
sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.inference: Credit card lending remains structurally subdued with no near-term recovery visible. Lenders should plan for a prolonged low-growth regime and prioritise yield optimisation over volume expansion through H1 FY27.

sibc-pl-gold-pos-streak.title: Gold loans positive for 8 straight periods — longest expansion ever

sibc-pl-gold-share.title: Gold loans at 7.02% share — highest on record, doubled in 15 months
sibc-pl-gold-share.observation: Gold loans now account for 7.02% of personal loans, the highest share in this 7-period window.

The gradual deceleration suggests the explosive phase is plateauing, but the 121% pace still far outstrips all other segments. Lenders with gold loan capacity should continue to lean in — this is a multi-year structural shift, not a fad.

sibc-pl-housing-share.title: Housing share falls to 48.2% — lowest on record

Vehicles at 10.7% are a reliable growth contributor. Credit cards at 4.3% are strengthening. Lenders should rebalance toward gold and vehicles to capture incremental flows.

Lenders should prioritize gold, vehicles, and other PL for incremental capital deployment.

sector_mix.narrative: Services credit grew 18.6% YoY in April 2026, decelerating for the first time after three periods of acceleration — the March 19.0% peak was not sustained. Within services, growth is concentrating in Computer Software (52.7%) and NBFCs (27.7%), while logistics and commerce sub-sectors have cooled. Trade credit is rebalancing: Retail is gaining share (44.4%, up from 43.6%) and shifted to strengthening status, reversing Wholesale's prior dominance, though Wholesale still grows 2.3× faster at 18.1% YoY versus Retail's 8.0%. The sector-wide story is one of moderation — the broad-based acceleration seen in March has narrowed to a few high-growth pockets, and lenders should tilt exposure toward technology and financial intermediation while monitoring Trade and logistics for further softening.

sibc-agriculture-share.title: Agriculture share steady at 12.44% — holding median

sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.title: Industry credit grew 15.0% in FY26 — 6.8pp step-up

This is the strongest industrial credit cycle in recent years, confirming a decisive re-rating of the sector.
sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.inference: Industry credit has broken out of its multi-year slowdown — the 6.8pp acceleration confirms a capex-driven recovery. Lenders should prioritize sectors with strong order books, balance sheet repair, and PLI-linked demand. This is the most compelling industrial lending opportunity in years.

chain: Industry credit grew 15.06% YoY in Apr 2026, up 0.04pp from 15.02% last period — effectively flat at the peak.
The five-period climb from 8.2% to 15.1% confirms a sustained breakout from the prior slowdown.
The plateau at 15% suggests the acceleration phase may be stabilizing, but momentum remains strong.
sibc-industry-yoy.inference: Industry lending has reached a new high and is holding there — the sustained 15% pace confirms a capex-driven recovery is entrenched. HFCs remain the larger segment, but PFIs are gaining ground — watch for a share crossover if PFI growth sustains. Lenders should assess exposure to both segments as the mix rebalances.

sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.title: PFIs surge 57.1% YoY — 40pp ahead of HFCs
sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Public Financial Institutions grew 57.1% YoY in Apr 2026, while Housing Finance Companies grew 16.7%.
sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.direction: PFIs accelerated from 52.5% to 57.1%, widening the spread from 37.0pp to 40.4pp — the largest gap in this scan.
chain: PFIs grew 57.1% YoY, accelerating 4.6pp from 52.5% last period.
HFCs grew 16.7%, up 1.2pp from 15.5%, but remain far behind PFIs in pace.
The 40.4pp spread is the widest on record, signaling PFIs are in a breakout phase while HFCs grow steadily but slowly.
sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.inference: PFIs are sustaining explosive growth — likely driven by government-backed infrastructure lending or policy-driven credit expansion. HFCs are stable but lagging. Lenders should assess PFI asset quality and concentration risk given the sustained high-velocity expansion.

sibc-personal-loans-share.title: Personal loans at 33.0% share — back to upper range
sibc-personal-loans-share.observation: Personal loans account for 32.98% of total bank credit in Apr 2026.
sibc-personal-loans-share.direction: Share rose 0.38pp from 32.59% last period, climbing from the bottom of the range (32.6%) to above the 75th percentile (33.0%).
chain: Personal loans share is 32.98%, up 0.38pp from 32.59% in Mar 2026.
The share rebounded from its lowest reading to the upper end of the 32.6%–33.0% range.
This reverses the prior period's decline, signaling personal loans are regaining ground in the credit mix.
sibc-personal-loans-share.inference: Personal loans have stopped losing share and are back near the top of their range — the rebalancing toward productive sectors has paused. Lenders should watch whether this is a temporary bounce or a sustained shift back to retail-heavy portfolios.

sibc-personal-loans-yoy.title: Personal loans 16.0% YoY — holding near peak
sibc-personal-loans-yoy.observation: Personal loans credit grew 16.01% YoY in Apr 2026.
sibc-personal-loans-yoy.direction: Growth eased 0.20pp from 16.21% last period, remaining above the 75th percentile (16.0%) and near the highest rate in this window.
chain: Personal loans grew 16.01% YoY, down marginally from 16.21% in Mar 2026.
Growth remains in the upper range (11.7%–16.0%), sustaining the climb from 11.7% in Mar 2025.
The sector is expanding robustly despite the slight deceleration, holding near its peak pace.
sibc-personal-loans-yoy.inference: Personal loans remain a growth engine, sustaining high-teens expansion. The marginal slowdown suggests the sector may be plateauing after a strong run. Lenders should monitor unsecured exposure — especially credit cards and personal loans — as growth stabilizes at elevated levels.

sibc-services-fy-acceleration.title: Services credit 19% in FY26 — 7pp step-up from FY25
sibc-services-fy-acceleration.observation: Services credit grew 12.0% in FY25 and 19.0% in FY26, marking a 7.0-point acceleration.
sibc-services-fy-acceleration.direction: The full-year acceleration measured at Mar 2026 year-end is 7.0pp, the sharpest annual step-up in this dataset.
chain: Services credit grew 12.0% in FY25 and 19.0% in FY26, a 7.0-point step-up.
This is the largest full-year acceleration on record, reflecting a decisive re-acceleration in services lending.
The jump signals broad-based demand across trade, NBFCs, and other services, driven by consumption recovery and NBFC expansion.
sibc-services-fy-acceleration.inference: Services credit is in its strongest cycle in recent years — the 7-point acceleration confirms a structural shift, not a temporary spike. Lenders should lean into services exposure, particularly NBFCs and trade, while monitoring asset quality as the sector scales rapidly.

sibc-services-share.title: Services share 28.2% — down from Mar peak
sibc-services-share.observation: Services account for 28.21% of total bank credit in Apr 2026.
sibc-services-share.direction: Share declined 0.26pp from 28.47% last period, slipping from the top of the range (28.5%) but remaining above the 75th percentile (28.2%).
chain: Services share is 28.21%, down 0.26pp from 28.47% in Mar 2026.
The share retreated from its highest reading but remains in the upper range (27.8%–28.5%).
This suggests services are still gaining structural ground, but the pace of share capture has moderated.
sibc-services-share.inference: Services remain a dominant force in the credit mix, holding near the top of their range despite the slight pullback. The sector is still capturing a larger slice of the pool than a year ago. Lenders should maintain services exposure but watch for signs of saturation as share stabilizes.

sibc-services-share-scan.title: NBFCs 34.5% of services — Trade weakens to 22.1%
sibc-services-share-scan.observation: Within services credit, NBFCs hold 34.5% share, Trade 22.1%, and Other Services 20.5%. Shipping, Computer Software, and Aviation are the smallest at 0.2%, 0.9%, and 0.9% respectively.
sibc-services-share-scan.direction: NBFCs shifted from strengthening to active status, holding their lead at 34.5% (up from 34.1%). Trade shifted from active to weakening, declining from 22.7% to 22.1%. The spread between top (NBFCs) and bottom (Shipping) widened to 34.4pp from 34.0pp.
chain: NBFCs hold 34.5% of services credit, up 0.4pp from 34.1%, but shifted from strengthening to active status.
Trade declined 0.6pp from 22.7% to 22.1% and shifted to weakening status, signaling slowing growth.
The 34.4pp spread is the widest in this scan, with NBFCs consolidating dominance while Trade loses ground.
sibc-services-share-scan.inference: NBFCs remain the dominant force in services credit, holding steady at 34.5% despite the status shift to active. Trade is losing momentum — the weakening status and share decline signal slowing demand, possibly due to softer consumption or inventory cycles. Lenders should prioritize NBFC exposure while monitoring Trade asset quality as growth decelerates.

sibc-services-yoy.title: Services credit 18.6% YoY — momentum easing from 19.0% peak
sibc-services-yoy.observation: Services credit grew 18.6% year-on-year in April 2026, down 0.42 percentage points from the prior period.
sibc-services-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated from 19.0% in March 2026 to 18.6% in April 2026. The trajectory over the past year shows: 12.0% → 15.5% → 16.3% → 19.0% → 18.6%.
chain: Services credit grew 18.6% YoY in April 2026, down from the 19.0% peak in March 2026.
This is the first deceleration after three consecutive periods of acceleration (12.0% → 15.5% → 16.3% → 19.0%).
The 18.6% rate remains at the upper end of the historical range (p75), indicating strong but no longer accelerating momentum.
The deceleration suggests the March peak may have been a year-end push; April's rate tests whether the underlying trend holds or softens further.
sibc-services-yoy.inference: Services credit remains the fastest-growing major sector, but the momentum has peaked. The 0.42-point pullback from March's high is the first sign of moderation after a sustained run-up. Lenders should watch the next period closely: if growth stabilises near 18–19%, the trend is intact; if it falls below 18%, the acceleration phase is over. Maintain services exposure but prepare for a slower growth regime.

sibc-services-yoy-scan.title: 6 of 10 services sub-sectors active — Computer Software 52.7% leads
sibc-services-yoy-scan.observation: All 10 services sub-sectors are growing year-on-year. Computer Software leads at 52.7%, followed by Shipping at 40.7% and NBFCs at 27.7%. Six sub-sectors are active, four are strengthening.
sibc-services-yoy-scan.direction: Status shifts reversed from the prior period: Computer Software moved from active to strengthening (52.7%), while Shipping, Tourism, Commercial Real Estate, and Trade all shifted from strengthening back to active. The spread between top and bottom widened to 44.1 percentage points — Computer Software at 52.7% versus Transport Operators at 8.7%.
chain: Computer Software credit surged to 52.7% YoY growth, up from 39.0% last period, and shifted to strengthening status.
Four previously strengthening sub-sectors (Shipping 40.7%, Tourism 21.0%, Commercial Real Estate 17.5%, Trade 13.4%) reverted to active status, indicating their acceleration has paused.
The 44.1-point spread between Computer Software (52.7%) and Transport Operators (8.7%) is the widest in this dataset, showing extreme divergence within services.
The shift from 8 strengthening sub-sectors last period to 4 now signals that the broad-based acceleration has narrowed to a few high-growth pockets.
sibc-services-yoy-scan.inference: Services credit growth is concentrating in technology and financial intermediation — Computer Software (52.7%) and NBFCs (27.7%) are pulling away, while logistics and commerce sub-sectors have cooled. The reversal of four sub-sectors from strengthening to active confirms the March peak was not sustained across the board. Lenders should prioritise exposure to Computer Software and NBFCs, where momentum is still building, and reassess capacity in Shipping, Trade, and Tourism, where growth has plateaued. Transport Operators at 8.7% remains the weakest link — monitor for stress.

sibc-trade-sub-share-scan.title: Wholesale 55.6% of Trade credit — Retail gaining share
sibc-trade-sub-share-scan.observation: Within Trade credit, Wholesale holds 55.6% share and Retail 44.4%, an 11.1 percentage point spread. Wholesale is weakening while Retail is strengthening.
sibc-trade-sub-share-scan.direction: Status divergence reversed from the prior period: Wholesale shifted from strengthening to weakening, while Retail shifted from weakening to strengthening. Wholesale share declined from 56.4% to 55.6%, while Retail share rose from 43.6% to 44.4%.
chain: Wholesale Trade share fell 0.8 points (56.4% → 55.6%) and shifted to weakening status, while Retail Trade share rose 0.8 points (43.6% → 44.4%) and shifted to strengthening.
The status reversal indicates Retail is now capturing a larger share of incremental Trade credit, reversing the prior period's trend where Wholesale was pulling away.
The 11.1-point spread between Wholesale (55.6%) and Retail (44.4%) has narrowed from 12.7 points last period, confirming Retail's share gain is structural, not noise.
sibc-trade-sub-share-scan.inference: Retail Trade is clawing back share within the Trade pool, reversing the prior period's Wholesale dominance. This shift suggests consumer-facing commerce is regaining momentum, possibly driven by festive demand or e-commerce financing. Lenders should rebalance Trade exposure: Wholesale remains the larger segment but is losing ground, while Retail is the share gainer. Monitor whether Retail's strengthening status persists — if it does, it signals a structural shift toward consumer credit within Trade.

sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.title: Wholesale 18.1% YoY, Retail 8.0% — both active, gap narrowing
sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Wholesale Trade credit grew 18.1% year-on-year and Retail Trade grew 8.0%, both active. The growth spread between them is 10.1 percentage points.
sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Both sub-sectors shifted from strengthening to active status, indicating their acceleration has paused. The growth spread narrowed from 8.5 points last period (20.1% vs 11.6%) to 10.1 points now (18.1% vs 8.0%), driven by Wholesale decelerating faster than Retail.
chain: Wholesale Trade growth decelerated from 20.1% to 18.1% (down 2.0 points), while Retail Trade growth decelerated from 11.6% to 8.0% (down 3.6 points).
Both sub-sectors shifted from strengthening to active, confirming the March acceleration was not sustained.
Despite the deceleration, Wholesale still grows 2.3× faster than Retail in percentage terms (18.1% vs 8.0%), maintaining its dominance in absolute growth.
The 10.1-point spread, though wider than last period's 8.5 points, reflects Retail's sharper deceleration, not Wholesale pulling away.
sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Trade credit growth is cooling across both Wholesale and Retail, but Wholesale retains a commanding growth advantage at 18.1% versus Retail's 8.0%. The shift from strengthening to active for both sub-sectors confirms the March peak was a temporary spike, not a new trend. Lenders should prioritise Wholesale Trade underwriting — it is decelerating but still growing at double Retail's pace. Retail's 8.0% growth is the weakest reading in this dataset; if it falls further, it signals demand saturation in consumer-facing commerce. Watch for Retail to stabilise or weaken further in the next period.

industry.narrative: Industry credit is undergoing a structural reallocation: Micro & Small enterprises surged 32.7% in FY26 vs 8.9% in FY25, capturing 3.1pp of share in 12 months, while large corporates lost 3.8pp despite re-accelerating to 10.0% YoY. The MSME vs large corporate growth spread stands at 20.1pp — extreme but narrowing as large corporates recover. Within Textiles, all four sub-segments are growing, but momentum is concentrating in Jute (19.9% YoY) and Other (13.3%), while Cotton lags at 4.8%. The incremental credit opportunity is overwhelmingly in smaller borrowers — lenders overweight to large corporates are missing the growth.

sibc-basic-metal-sub-share-scan.title: Iron & Steel share slips to 68.2% — Other Metal gains ground
sibc-basic-metal-sub-share-scan.observation: Iron and Steel holds 68.2% of Basic Metal credit, while Other Metal and Metal Products accounts for 31.8%.
sibc-basic-metal-sub-share-scan.direction: Iron and Steel's share declined from 68.8% → 68.2%, shifting from strengthening to weakening status. Other Metal and Metal Products rose from 31.2% → 31.8%, moving from weakening to strengthening. The spread narrowed from 37.7pp to 36.4pp.
chain: Iron and Steel's share fell 0.6pp to 68.2%, reversing its prior strengthening trend.
Other Metal and Metal Products gained 0.6pp to 31.8%, flipping from weakening to strengthening status.
The 36.4pp spread shows Iron and Steel still dominates, but the gap is narrowing as Other Metal gains momentum.
sibc-basic-metal-sub-share-scan.inference: The share reversal signals a tactical shift — lenders are diversifying within Basic Metal, backing non-ferrous metals and fabricated products after a period of steel concentration. Iron and Steel remains the core exposure, but Other Metal's strengthening status suggests emerging demand in aluminium, copper, and downstream metal products.

sibc-basic-metal-sub-yoy-scan.title: Other Metal 19.9% YoY — outpaces Iron & Steel at 16.7%
sibc-basic-metal-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Other Metal and Metal Products grew 19.9% year-on-year, while Iron and Steel expanded 16.7%.
sibc-basic-metal-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Other Metal and Metal Products leads growth by 3.3 percentage points. Iron and Steel shifted from strengthening to active status, moderating from 18.8% → 16.7%.
chain: Other Metal and Metal Products grew 19.9% YoY, maintaining its growth lead over Iron and Steel at 16.7%.
Iron and Steel's growth decelerated 2.1pp (from 18.8% to 16.7%), shifting from strengthening to active.
Both segments remain in expansion, but the 3.3pp spread confirms Other Metal is pulling ahead despite its smaller base.
sibc-basic-metal-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Other Metal's faster growth and Iron and Steel's deceleration confirm the share shift is driven by real demand divergence, not just base effects. Lenders should watch for sustained momentum in non-ferrous metals — if Other Metal holds above 19% for another period, it signals a structural rotation toward downstream fabrication and specialty alloys.

sibc-chemicals-sub-share-scan.title: Pharma share dips to 33.5% — Fertiliser stable at 10.2%
sibc-chemicals-sub-share-scan.observation: Within Chemicals, Others holds 44.1%, Drugs and Pharmaceuticals 33.5%, Petro Chemicals 12.2%, and Fertiliser 10.2%.
sibc-chemicals-sub-share-scan.direction: Drugs and Pharmaceuticals declined from 33.9% → 33.5%, shifting from strengthening to active. Fertiliser rose from 9.9% → 10.2%, moving from weakening to active. The spread narrowed from 34.3pp to 33.8pp.
chain: Drugs and Pharmaceuticals lost 0.4pp share to 33.5%, reversing its prior strengthening trend.
Fertiliser gained 0.3pp to 10.2%, stabilising after prior weakness.
All four segments are now active — no entity is strengthening or weakening, signalling balanced growth across Chemicals.
sibc-chemicals-sub-share-scan.inference: Pharma's share loss and status shift to active suggests its growth premium is fading — lenders are no longer concentrating exposure there. Fertiliser's stabilisation at 10.2% indicates the worst of the contraction may be over, but it remains the smallest segment. With all four entities now active, Chemicals credit is broadening rather than concentrating.

sibc-chemicals-sub-yoy-scan.title: Pharma surges to 20.6% YoY — Fertiliser contracts -0.3%
sibc-chemicals-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Drugs and Pharmaceuticals grew 20.6% year-on-year, Petro Chemicals 19.4%, and Others 16.4%. Fertiliser contracted -0.3%.
sibc-chemicals-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Drugs and Pharmaceuticals accelerated from 17.9% → 20.6%, shifting from active to strengthening. Others accelerated from 14.9% → 16.4%, moving from active to strengthening. Fertiliser improved from -4.5% → -0.3%, but remains in decline. The spread narrowed from 32.3pp to 20.9pp.
chain: Drugs and Pharmaceuticals grew 20.6% YoY, a 2.7pp acceleration, flipping to strengthening status.
Fertiliser's contraction moderated sharply from -4.5% to -0.3%, nearing stabilisation.
The 20.9pp spread is the narrowest in this window, driven by Fertiliser's recovery and broad-based acceleration across Pharma, Petro, and Others.
sibc-chemicals-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Pharma's 20.6% growth and strengthening status confirm robust healthcare demand — despite losing share, absolute credit expansion is accelerating. Fertiliser's near-zero contraction signals the structural stress is easing, likely on policy support or input cost relief. The narrowing spread and two strengthening entities point to broad-based momentum across Chemicals, with Pharma leading the charge.

sibc-engineering-sub-share-scan.title: Electronics share jumps to 19.9% — Others slip to 80.1%
sibc-engineering-sub-share-scan.observation: Within All Engineering, Others (non-Electronics) holds 80.1% share, while Electronics accounts for 19.9%.
sibc-engineering-sub-share-scan.direction: Others declined from 81.1% → 80.1%, shifting from strengthening to weakening. Electronics rose from 18.9% → 19.9%, moving from weakening to strengthening. The spread narrowed from 62.2pp to 60.2pp.
chain: Electronics gained 1.0pp share to 19.9%, reversing its prior weakening trend.
Others lost 1.0pp to 80.1%, flipping from strengthening to weakening status.
The 60.2pp spread shows Others still dominates, but Electronics is clawing back share after a period of underperformance.
sibc-engineering-sub-share-scan.inference: Electronics' 1.0pp share gain and status flip to strengthening signals renewed lender confidence — likely driven by semiconductor policy, PLI incentives, or consumer electronics demand. Others' weakening status suggests the capex-heavy engineering surge may be plateauing. If Electronics holds above 19.5% for another period, it confirms a structural rotation toward tech hardware and electronics manufacturing.

sibc-engineering-sub-yoy-scan.title: Others 34.5% YoY — Electronics strengthens to 17.8%
sibc-engineering-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Others (non-Electronics) grew 34.5% year-on-year, while Electronics expanded 17.8%.
sibc-engineering-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Others decelerated from 37.5% → 34.5%, but remains active. Electronics accelerated from 13.3% → 17.8%, shifting from active to strengthening. The spread narrowed from 24.2pp to 16.7pp.
chain: Others grew 34.5% YoY, a 3.0pp deceleration, but still leads absolute growth.
Electronics accelerated 4.5pp to 17.8%, flipping to strengthening status.
The 16.7pp spread is the narrowest in this window, driven by Electronics' acceleration and Others' moderation.
sibc-engineering-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Electronics' 17.8% growth and strengthening status confirm the share gain is backed by real demand acceleration, not just base effects. Others' 34.5% growth remains strong but is decelerating — the capex boom in heavy engineering is still robust, but the pace is moderating. The narrowing spread and Electronics' strengthening status suggest a rebalancing within Engineering credit, with tech hardware catching up to traditional machinery and capital goods.

sibc-food-processing-sub-share-scan.title: Food Processing: Others 75.9%, Sugar slips to weakening at 11.1%
sibc-food-processing-sub-share-scan.observation: Within Food Processing, Others holds 75.9%, Sugar 11.1%, Edible Oils and Vanaspati 11.1%, and Tea 1.9%.
sibc-food-processing-sub-share-scan.direction: Sugar shifted from active to weakening; Others moved from weakening to active. The spread is 74.0 percentage points.
chain: Sugar's share fell from 11.9% to 11.1% and shifted to weakening status — the first sign of structural loss within Food Processing.
Others regained active status at 75.9%, up from 75.6%, absorbing share from Sugar's decline.
Edible Oils held 11.1% and shifted to strengthening, matching Sugar's share for the first time — a compositional inflection point.
sibc-food-processing-sub-share-scan.inference: Sugar is losing ground within Food Processing — likely on weak realisations, inventory overhang, or policy headwinds. Edible Oils now matches Sugar's share and is strengthening — lenders should watch for Oils overtaking Sugar in the next period. Others' return to active status signals broad-based credit demand across smaller Food Processing segments.

sibc-food-processing-sub-yoy-scan.title: Edible Oils 33.0% YoY leads Food Processing; Sugar turns negative at -0.4%
sibc-food-processing-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Edible Oils and Vanaspati grew 33.0% year-on-year, Others 13.6%, Sugar -0.4%, and Tea -3.1%.
sibc-food-processing-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Sugar shifted from active to declining at -0.4%; Tea moved from strengthening to declining at -3.1%. Edible Oils accelerated from 25.3% to 33.0%. The spread is 36.1 percentage points.
chain: Edible Oils surged from 25.3% to 33.0% YoY and shifted to strengthening — the fastest growth within Food Processing and a 7.7-point acceleration.
Sugar turned negative at -0.4%, shifting from active to declining — the first contraction in this dataset.
Tea fell from 0.8% to -3.1% and shifted to declining — a 3.9-point deceleration and the weakest reading among Food Processing sub-segments.
sibc-food-processing-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Edible Oils is the clear growth engine within Food Processing, driven by import substitution, rising consumption, and capacity expansion — lenders should prioritise exposure here. Sugar's turn to contraction signals sector stress — weak prices, inventory buildup, or policy uncertainty. Tea's shift to declining confirms the prior period's recovery was short-lived — avoid new commitments until growth stabilises.

sibc-industry-type-share-scan.title: Infrastructure 32.8% of industry credit — 19 segments all active, stable composition
sibc-industry-type-share-scan.observation: Infrastructure accounts for 32.8% of industry credit, Basic Metal and Metal Products 11.3%, and Other Industries 7.5%. Leather and Leather Products holds just 0.3%.
sibc-industry-type-share-scan.direction: Infrastructure's share rose from 32.5% to 32.8%. All 19 industry types remain in active status. The spread is 32.5 percentage points.
chain: Infrastructure's share increased 0.3 points to 32.8% — the highest in this dataset and nearly three times the next-largest segment.
All 19 segments remain active for the second consecutive period — no status shifts, signaling stable compositional dynamics.
The spread of 32.5 points is unchanged from 32.2 points — Infrastructure's dominance is entrenched.
sibc-industry-type-share-scan.inference: Infrastructure's rising share confirms sustained capex in power, roads, and utilities — lenders should maintain overweight exposure. The absence of status shifts across all 19 segments signals broad-based but uneven growth — no major structural reallocation is underway. Leather and Leather Products at 0.3% remains a negligible segment with no strategic relevance.

sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.title: Petroleum 37.8% YoY leads industry; 10 of 19 segments now strengthening
sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.observation: Petroleum, Coal Products and Nuclear Fuels grew 37.8% year-on-year, All Engineering 30.8%, and Gems and Jewellery 26.0%. Leather and Leather Products grew just 6.4%.
sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.direction: Petroleum accelerated from 32.4% to 37.8% and shifted to strengthening. Gems and Jewellery decelerated from 41.4% to 26.0% and shifted from strengthening to active. Ten of 19 segments are now strengthening, up from twelve. The spread is 31.3 percentage points.
chain: Petroleum surged to 37.8% YoY — a 5.4-point acceleration from 32.4% and the fastest growth across all 19 industry types.
Gems and Jewellery decelerated sharply from 41.4% to 26.0% — a 15.4-point slowdown — and shifted from strengthening to active, losing its prior leadership position.

Ten segments are strengthening, down from twelve — Chemicals and Paper shifted from active to strengthening, but Gems, Basic Metal, and Glass shifted from strengthening to active, signaling a narrowing of momentum.
sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.inference: Petroleum is the new growth leader, driven by energy transition capex and refining capacity expansion — lenders should prioritise exposure. Gems and Jewellery's sharp deceleration from 41.4% to 26.0% signals cooling export demand or working capital normalisation — watch for further slowdown. The decline in strengthening segments from twelve to ten suggests growth is concentrating in fewer pockets — broad-based momentum is weakening.

sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.title: Power 56.8% of Infrastructure; Telecom slips to weakening at 6.6%
sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.observation: Within Infrastructure, Power holds 56.8%, Roads 22.8%, and Other Infrastructure 12.2%. Railways (other than Indian Railways) accounts for just 0.4%.
sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.direction: Power's share rose from 56.5% to 56.8%. Telecommunications shifted from active to weakening at 6.6%. The spread is 56.4 percentage points.
chain: Power's share increased 0.3 points to 56.8% — the highest in this dataset and more than double Roads' 22.8%.
Telecommunications fell from active to weakening at 6.6% — the first status shift in this segment, signaling structural share loss within Infrastructure.
Six of seven segments remain active; only Telecom is weakening — Power's dominance is strengthening while Telecom contracts.
sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.inference: Power's rising share confirms sustained investment in renewable energy and thermal capacity — lenders should maintain overweight exposure. Telecom's shift to weakening signals sector stress, deleveraging, or lender exits — avoid new commitments until share stabilises. Roads at 22.8% remains the second-largest segment but is not gaining ground — growth is concentrating in Power.

sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.title: Ports 74.0% YoY surges; Railways -44.1%, Airports -26.0% in sharp contraction
sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Ports grew 74.0% year-on-year, Power 21.5%, and Other Infrastructure 1.4%. Railways (other than Indian Railways) contracted -44.1%, Airports -26.0%, and Telecommunications -11.9%.
sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Ports accelerated from 48.7% to 74.0% and shifted to strengthening. Railways deepened from -49.8% to -44.1% but remains in decline. The spread is 118.1 percentage points — the widest in this domain.
chain: Ports surged from 48.7% to 74.0% YoY — a 25.3-point acceleration and the fastest growth within Infrastructure.
Railways contracted -44.1%, Airports -26.0%, and Telecom -11.9% — three of seven segments are declining, unchanged from the prior period.
The spread widened from 98.5 to 118.1 percentage points — the largest divergence in this dataset, signaling extreme polarisation within Infrastructure credit.
sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Ports is the standout growth driver within Infrastructure, likely on trade volume recovery, capacity expansion, and logistics capex — lenders should prioritise exposure. Railways and Airports remain in sharp contraction — project delays, refinancing stress, or lender exits — avoid new commitments until growth stabilises. The 118.1-point spread confirms extreme polarisation: Power and Ports are surging while transport segments collapse.

sibc-large-corporate-share.title: Large corporate share slips to 67.2% — lowest in 7 periods
sibc-large-corporate-share.observation: Large corporates account for 67.2% of industry credit in Apr 2026, down 0.08pp from the prior period.
sibc-large-corporate-share.direction: Share compressed from 71.0% in Jan 2025 to 67.2% in Apr 2026, losing 3.8 percentage points over six periods.
chain: Large corporate share fell from 71.0% to 67.2% over six periods — a 3.8pp erosion.
The current 67.2% is the lowest reading in this seven-period window, near the p25 of 67.1%.
MSME segments are capturing the share that large corporates are losing — the reallocation is structural and accelerating.
sibc-large-corporate-share.inference: Large corporates are losing their grip on industry credit. The 3.8pp share loss in six periods is the sharpest structural shift in this dataset. Lenders overweight to large corporates are missing the growth — incremental credit is flowing to MSME borrowers.

sibc-large-corporate-yoy.title: Large corporate credit hits 10.0% YoY — fastest in 7 periods
sibc-large-corporate-yoy.observation: Large corporate credit grew 10.0% YoY in Apr 2026, the strongest reading in this seven-period window.
sibc-large-corporate-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 9.1% last period to 10.0%, extending a recovery that began at 5.5% in Jan 2026.
chain: Large corporate credit grew 10.0% YoY in Apr 2026, up from 9.1% in Mar 2026 — the fastest pace in seven periods.
The 10.0% rate is at the p75 bound, marking a sustained recovery from the 5.5% low in Jan 2026.
Despite this acceleration, large corporates are growing far slower than MSME segments, which are expanding above 20%.
sibc-large-corporate-yoy.inference: Large corporates are re-accelerating, but the 10.0% pace is insufficient to defend market share against 30%+ MSME growth. The segment is healthy but structurally outpaced — lenders here are capturing single-digit share of incremental industry credit.

sibc-msme-medium-share.title: Medium enterprises hold 9.7% share — highest on record
sibc-msme-medium-share.observation: Medium enterprises account for 9.7% of industry credit in Apr 2026, up 0.07pp from the prior period.
sibc-msme-medium-share.direction: Share rose from 8.9% in Jan 2025 to 9.7% by Jan 2026 and has held at 9.7% for four consecutive periods.
chain: Medium enterprise share reached 9.7% in Apr 2026, the highest level in this seven-period window.
The segment gained 0.8pp from Jan 2025 to Jan 2026, then defended that position for four straight periods.
Medium enterprises are growing fast enough to maintain their foothold but not expanding further — the share has plateaued.
sibc-msme-medium-share.inference: Medium enterprises have carved out a 9.7% share and are holding it. The four-period plateau signals the segment is growing in line with the broader industry pool, not outpacing it. Lenders here are capturing steady flow, not outsized growth.

sibc-msme-medium-yoy.title: Medium enterprises at 19.9% YoY — moderating from 21.8%
sibc-msme-medium-yoy.observation: Medium enterprise credit grew 19.9% YoY in Apr 2026, down 1.96pp from the prior period.
sibc-msme-medium-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated from 21.8% in Mar 2026 to 19.9%, after peaking at 22.3% in Jan 2026.
chain: Medium enterprise credit grew 19.9% YoY in Apr 2026, down from 21.8% in Mar 2026.
The segment sustained above 20% growth for three periods (Jan–Mar 2026), but has now slipped below that threshold.
Despite the deceleration, 19.9% is still double the large corporate pace of 10.0%.
sibc-msme-medium-yoy.inference: Medium enterprises are decelerating but remain a strong outperformer. The 19.9% pace is healthy and far ahead of large corporates, but the momentum is softening. Lenders should watch for further deceleration — if growth drops below 18%, the segment risks losing share.

sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.title: Micro & Small surged 32.7% in FY26 vs 8.9% in FY25 — 23.8pp step-up
sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.observation: Micro & Small credit grew 8.9% in the year ending Mar 2025 and 32.7% in the year ending Mar 2026, a 23.8 percentage point acceleration.
sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.direction: Full-year growth jumped from 8.9% in FY25 to 32.7% in FY26 — the sharpest year-over-year step-up in this dataset.
chain: Micro & Small credit grew 8.9% in FY25 and 32.7% in FY26 — a 23.8pp acceleration, the largest in this dataset.
The segment shifted from single-digit growth to 30%+ expansion in one year, signaling a structural inflection.
This is not incremental improvement — it is a regime change, likely driven by policy tailwinds, digital underwriting scale, or supply chain financing expansion.
sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.inference: This is the defining inflection in industry credit. Micro & Small lending has entered a new growth regime, and lenders with exposure here are riding the fastest-growing segment. The 23.8pp step-up is unprecedented — banks and NBFCs scaling this segment are capturing outsized share of incremental credit.

sibc-msme-micro-small-share.title: Micro & Small hold 23.1% share — up 3.1pp in 12 months
sibc-msme-micro-small-share.observation: Micro & Small enterprises account for 23.1% of industry credit in Apr 2026, up 0.01pp from the prior period.
sibc-msme-micro-small-share.direction: Share surged from 20.0% in Mar 2025 to 23.5% in Jan 2026, then eased to 23.1% — a net gain of 3.1 percentage points in 12 months.
chain: Micro & Small share rose from 20.0% in Mar 2025 to 23.1% in Apr 2026, a 3.1pp gain in 12 months.
The segment captured share at the expense of large corporates, which lost 3.8pp over the same period.
The 3pp shift in one year is the largest structural reallocation in this dataset — banks and NBFCs are aggressively scaling down-market.
sibc-msme-micro-small-share.inference: Micro & Small borrowers are capturing share at the expense of large corporates. The 3.1pp gain in 12 months is a structural reallocation, not noise. Lenders focused on large corporates are being left behind — the incremental credit opportunity is overwhelmingly in smaller borrowers.

sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.title: Micro & Small at 30.1% YoY — 3× large corporate pace
sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.observation: Micro & Small credit grew 30.1% YoY in Apr 2026, down 2.59pp from the prior period.
sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated from 32.7% in Mar 2026 to 30.1%, after exploding from 8.9% in Mar 2025 to 31.2% in Jan 2026.
chain: Micro & Small credit grew 30.1% YoY in Apr 2026, down from 32.7% in Mar 2026.
The segment has sustained above 30% growth for four consecutive periods (Jan–Apr 2026), after growing below 10% in early 2025.
At 30.1%, Micro & Small is growing three times faster than large corporates at 10.0%.
sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.inference: This is the hottest segment in industry credit. Four straight periods above 30% signals entrenched structural demand, not a one-time spike. Lenders with Micro & Small exposure are seeing 3× the growth of large corporates — the trend is structural and accelerating.

sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.title: MSME outpacing large corporates by 20.1pp — spread narrowing
sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.observation: MSME credit is growing 20.1 percentage points faster than large corporate credit in Apr 2026.
sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.direction: The spread narrowed from 23.5pp last period to 20.1pp, after widening from 2.1pp in Mar 2025 to 25.7pp in Jan 2026.
chain: The MSME vs large corporate growth spread narrowed from 23.5pp in Mar 2026 to 20.1pp in Apr 2026.
The narrowing is driven by large corporates accelerating to 10.0% while MSME growth moderated from 32.7% to 30.1%.
Despite the narrowing, the 20.1pp spread remains extreme — MSME is still growing 3× faster than large corporates.
sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.inference: The growth divergence between MSME and large corporates is narrowing but remains extreme. Large corporates are re-accelerating, but MSME segments are still capturing the bulk of incremental credit. Lenders overweight to large corporates are missing the growth — the 20.1pp spread confirms the incremental opportunity is in smaller borrowers.

sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.title: Other Textiles leads at 44.7% — Cotton second at 36.5%
sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.observation: Within Textiles, Other Textiles accounts for 44.7% of credit, Cotton Textiles 36.5%, Man-Made 17.1%, and Jute 1.7%.
sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.direction: The four sub-segments span a 42.9 percentage point range, with Other Textiles and Cotton together holding 81.2% of the pool.
chain: Other Textiles accounts for 44.7% of Textiles credit, Cotton Textiles 36.5%, Man-Made 17.1%, and Jute 1.7%.
Other and Cotton together hold 81.2% of the pool — the segment is concentrated in two legacy categories.
Man-Made and Jute are marginal at 17.1% and 1.7% respectively — any shift in Cotton or Other will move the entire sector.
sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.inference: Textiles credit is concentrated in Other and Cotton, which together account for 81.2% of the pool. Lenders with exposure here are betting on legacy categories — Man-Made and Jute are too small to matter. Any credit shift in Other or Cotton will drive the entire sector.

sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.title: Jute leads Textiles at 19.9% YoY — Other at 13.3%, Cotton at 4.8%
sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Within Textiles, Jute credit grew 19.9% YoY, Other Textiles 13.3%, Cotton Textiles 4.8%, and Man-Made 2.7%.
sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Other Textiles and Man-Made both shifted from strengthening to active status this period, while Jute and Cotton remain strengthening. The spread across the four segments is 17.2 percentage points.
chain: Jute credit grew 19.9% YoY, the fastest in Textiles, followed by Other at 13.3%, Cotton at 4.8%, and Man-Made at 2.7%.
Other Textiles and Man-Made both shifted from strengthening to active status, signaling momentum moderation.
All four segments are growing, but the 17.2pp spread shows Jute and Other are pulling away from Cotton and Man-Made.
sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Textiles credit is in a broad-based recovery, but momentum is concentrating in Jute and Other. The two largest segments — Other (44.7% share) and Cotton (36.5% share) — are growing at 13.3% and 4.8% respectively, with Other decelerating. Lenders should watch Cotton closely — at 4.8%, it is the weakest link in the recovery.
